## 1. Introduction

There are many factors which influence consumer choices when purchasing products. One important factor is consumer trust in the firm with which they are doing business. When consumers trust a firm, they believe that the firm has the ability to produce quality products at a low cost. At a more interpersonal level, consumers trust that the firm will act in the best interest of the consumer by providing a quality product and information about the product. As a result, consumers are more willing to engage in relationships with trusted firms. Donaldson and Dunfee define trust as belief in the reliability of another; trust occurs when one party has acceptable evidence that its welfare will not be sacrificed at the behest of the other party. Trust, in turn, may lead to economic benefits for the firm in the form of satisfied repeat customers, favorable word-of-mouth marketing, and resistance to discontinuing the use of the firm's goods.

### 1.1. Background and Significance

Unethical practices in the business environment can have a significant impact on consumers' trust toward the company and lead to negative consumer behavior, especially towards the company or its products. Unethical practices are actions or activities that stakeholders - especially consumers, but also the company's employees and management - consider unacceptable. If the actions are seen as highly unethical, then they are usually regarded as immoral as well. In addition to classic tools of influencing consumer behavior, such as advertising messages, public relations activities, or sales promotion, modern companies often use event marketing for communications and building a brand. It is assumed that good event marketing communications also influence consumer perceptions and trust. These practices occur at a time when the business environment, the global public, and other stakeholders consider ethical issues to be more and more important in the business-based decisions and activities of companies and broader business processes. Unethical conduct by business organizations is becoming less and less acceptable. Despite the formal protection and guarantees of consumer interests and the fact that consumer trust is seen as one of the most important sources of the company's value, the number of scandalous cases of unethical conduct of well-known companies is on the rise. These cases are directly linked to a sexual scandal, causing damage to a company that violates stakeholder trust, but also the public's trust, which is the foundation stone of the company's credibility. Consumer trust is considered to be highly vulnerable and quite fragile. By unethical conduct, a company creates preconditions for losing trust to be damaged - and not just regarding a certain product or service. The negative consequences have wider boundaries. In addition to losing trust, there are other negative consequences. Unethical conduct negatively influences consumers' willingness to remain loyal and to continue to remain in a business relationship and has a significant impact on consumers' decisions to boycott the company.

### 1.2. Research Objectives

The main objective of my research is to investigate the correlation existing between perceived unethical business practices and specific issues regarding consumers' trust, be it in terms of the company's reliability, goodwill, or perceived dedication to delivering value and satisfying the customers' needs. Prior studies have demonstrated that perceived company's prestige, ability, optimism, and status are very significant dimensions in describing consumer trust. Furthermore, the quality of individual interactions between the company and its customers and the level of dependence existing between both parties can directly or indirectly affect company prestige, reliability, status, goodwill, and dedication. On the basis of the above, I structured my research in an innovative way. More details about the conceptual background and the hypotheses will be presented in the following sections. In total, we set out to study three specific research objectives. The first research objective aims to offer an answer to those in the academic world or in business practices who wonder to what degree consumers openly recognize the business entities that involve in specific ethical behaviors designed to meet social welfare guidelines. It has come to our attention that we lack sufficient information about the factors that greatly affect consumers to turn toward "green" action and what entity characteristics influence consumer-related behaviors in a green economy. This topic is all the more vital given the fact that, until now, there are only a few studies concentrating their attention on this idea, which explains consumer behavior in the eco-friendly consumption area. Our second business goal is facilities by the need for a sustainable solution to the environmental problems facing our society, finding a response to these self-imposed questions.

## 2. Theoretical Framework

Business practices impact not only the business outcomes and performance but also society. Companies have the ability to avoid negative societal and environmental implications by engaging in ethical practices. Unethical business practices lead to deteriorating the economic and social systems of the country, affect the operating environment of the business, the image, reputation, and satisfaction of consumers, and mostly influence stakeholders' trust. Ethical business practice is vital because unethical businesses have an impact on market outcomes and lead to various risks, including market failure. Today, ethical business practices are the subject of interest in corporate activities around the world. It's important to define commercial behavior in compliance with rules and policies, show the necessities of traditional and ethical approaches for stakeholders, and highlight the importance of company operations for society. It should be underlined that these ethical actions are essential not only for companies to establish honest, transparent, loyal, and reliable relationships with different interest groups but also for trust in companies. This is because people expect ethical behavior from these organizations to maintain and maintain their trust in the business world. The provision of services and the incorporation of ethical attitudes into professionals are priorities to maintain this trust. Unethical business practices by companies have the potential to reduce the trust of consumers in companies, which can lead to corporate losses and bankruptcies. In the absence of feedback processes, such practices will not come as a loss to businesses or a reaction from consumers. But trust is an important characteristic that players in a financial relationship hope for. It is a strategy tool that will protect consumers from the regular risks they face. When people believe that real business intentions are trustworthy, they reveal their mutual intentions and measure their success. Based on these definitions, the possible effects of unethical business practices on consumer trust are discussed.

### 2.1. Consumer Trust

With the evolution of the complexity of the market, firms began to devote much effort to manage stakeholders. According to Freeman (1984), a stakeholder is any group or individual who can affect or is affected by the achievement of the organization's objectives. These are, for instance, shareholders, employees, consumers, suppliers, and government. Although financial performance continues to be an important aspect in organizational survival, social, ethical, and ecological performances also contribute to creating more positive, loyal, and committed stakeholders (Dowling and Pfeffer, 1975; Freeman, 1984). Recognition of 'business' as 'ethical' is one of the factors that motivate stakeholders, especially consumers, to be positively influenced, holding favorable perceptions toward ethical firms. High generalized trust is also reported by those who have higher generalized perceptions of others' honesty, which include business owners (Berger and Volgy, 2003) and promoting free enterprise (Grimm, 1988).

### 2.2. Unethical Business Practices

Unethical business practices which have been publicized have included insider trading, fraudulent sales, misstated financial reports, and falsified records. Although these acts are illegal, it can be postulated that the issue of business ethics has assumed greater importance than ever before, in the eyes of both the business community and the public. As business organizations have grown in size and power, the general public has become concerned over the negative influence which business organizations have on society. As a result, the business sector in the New Zealand economy has witnessed increasing levels of public scrutiny. Then for a number of businesses, ill-advised actions have been described as unethical, and the results of surveys and public opinion polls indicate that businesses have been long criticized for unethical behavior. Any negative behavior which results in a conflict between business and society has been termed by business ethicists as an unethical business practice. For the purpose of this study, the term unethical business practice is defined as any behavior that is not in accordance with what is considered morally right in business and which is recognized as such by society. The nature of an institution or its activities may be related to unequal power relationships in society, but the institution itself enjoys a measure of trust and credibility. As a result, in any abuse of integrity, the violation of trust is compounded by the dishonor of public duty as well as by an abuse of standing. In Western modern capitalist countries, the link between the growth and influence of business organizations and public perceptions of the morality of business is inappropriate means of achieving success.

The popular reporting of unethical management, albeit somewhat sensational, has clearly resonated with the public, as evidenced by the popularity of the various news programs devoted to these topics. ### 3.1. Historical Perspectives

Despite the many different forms that unethical behavior can take, there is a general problem with corporate ethics. This problem is at least partially determined by the nature of business operations. In the end, a company is not an ethical entity, the only humans are those who work there. So, if asked about the high interest in business ethics in the late twentieth century, it is because of the inconsistency between the quality of expectations of business practitioners and the actual behavior of business managers. Additionally, in most modern business practices, the actual central motivation is simply to earn profits, mostly in the short term. These bonds are regular to all employees of the company. For the manager who makes decisions, there is a double responsibility to earn returns for equity shareholders and not to betray what most customers desire, which is for firms to be good corporate citizens. Albeit a moderate interest in customer satisfaction and long-term profits, one can understand why there might be a general concern for ensuring that the more visible reflections of corporate citizenship, isn't for companies. It is no surprise, then, that businesses are their own worst enemies in persuading the public to politicize and directly supervise them, as well as allow themselves the autonomy to make important decisions that protect stakeholders' interests. For this purpose and other practical motives, corporations have sought carefully to regulate the consumption practices of consumers while seeking limited regulation from legislative bodies. However, the acceptance of this power position is very important for businessmen's wealth and political well-being. The impact of the business on consumers may be hard to control. Since the Public Welfare Period of the New Deal, there has been increasing distrust of big businesses, particularly in its manifestations. As most of the manifestations of unethical business activity are deliberate and fully reported and below, this scope mostly depends on the issue of whether unethical measures are in sight.

### 3.2. Key Concepts and Definitions

Values. Drucker (1997) argued that humans, as beings, possess ethical responsibility. They need values to make ethical choices and structure decision-making. Ethical values are ends, the things we want to achieve with our lives, and they are the foundation, the building blocks from which other kinds of values derive. Economic values are means; they are the things we value for what they contribute to economic well-being and for the freedom to not value them. They are vital, but they are not the goals. Ethical values occupy prominence and should affect strategic decisions in a business. Organizational culture within the company and the behavior of the leaders are crucial to building with the ethical values, in establishing clean moral standards, and to motivate and recruit employees who voluntarily commit themselves to compliance with such values and practices. Organizations must value ethics and develop organizational ethical cultures. Ethical Issues in Business. Business issues are different from ethical issues. Marketplace activities can directly affect the quality of life of the community; hence, business leaders and executives should steer clear of ethical paradoxes and conundrums. The most perplexing problems are due to the integration of multinational and diversified markets. Ethical problems are complex with some well-defined philosophical implications. Philosophers give us historical perspectives and consider the limitations of financial theory. The viewpoint of some theologians gives us perspectives on the dimensions of ethical enterprises. Business leaders need a unified understanding. Ethics. Ethics involves the process of determining right or wrong action in given circumstances. It is much different from what the law stipulates, which allows self-serving decisions contrary to intended settlements. Diverse cultures value and enforce different ethical standards. Approaches. Sociological studies differentiate five different ethical approaches to business: (1) instrumental and utilitarian; (2) political-legal; (3) economic; (4) human values; and (5) relativistic.

## 4. Methodology

This research aims to enlarge the current understanding of why consumers trust companies, focusing on the negative dynamics in building and maintaining consumer trust. The research deals with the influence of consumer perceived corporate unethical behavior on customer trust in a company. The basis of research results suggests that positive perception of a company as committed to stakeholders' interests is a foundation of loyalty. The results create opportunities for a broader understanding of the mechanisms guiding various links between the corporate reputation of a company's stakeholder relations and company success, and contribute to ongoing research on corporate reputation and ultimately a company's success. From a pure academic perspective, the research also promises a better understanding of the role of various stakeholders in the complexity of corporate reputation. The author's empirical results specifically address consumer perceptions of corporate reputation as an integrated construct: integrating the corporate behaviors, perceptions, and consumer personal values. The findings help to verify the assumption that relevant stakeholders play a significant role in building a strong corporate reputation, and confirm earlier works that pointed to the relationship between consumer trust and corporate reputation. The research adopts qualitative focus group research methodology. The objective is to examine how customers perceive corporate ethics and to understand the combination of factors comprising corporate reputation, which create consumer trust.

### 4.1. Research Design

The purpose of this study is to gather information and analyze the impact of certain business characteristics that consumers may consider unethical. The questions to be answered are: What unethical business characteristics exist and how do these affect consumers? The consumers' intention to buy and the firm's attempt to apply ethical advertising may be affected depending on whether the consumer trusts the industry, large-sized companies, the type of advertising, the firm's sector, and the public image of the profession. Other elements in the study have to do with the consumer's professional management of her own investments and the purchase of lottery tickets. Another significant factor concerning the study is the appeal that certain products possess (especially those that study authors wrap within the concept "sophisticated products") in relation to the degree of information or personal involvement in the process of analysis. The study suggests numerous lines of research that may be conducted in the future. Unethical business activities that serve to manipulate earnings or share valuations, such as unaccounted for public relations activities or official corporate announcements, may substantially affect stakeholders. Consumers should be informed about these manipulations. The authors appreciate the difficulties involved in these matters and are grateful for the thoughtful input provided by the Journal reviewers.

### 4.2. Data Collection and Analysis

The data for this research was collected in three rounds of interviews. In the first round of interviews, eight nonprofit industry leaders (associations) with top management experience in their associations' for-profit industry members, as well as insight and knowledge of their members' business practices, were consulted about the most prevalent unethical business practices or behavior in the industries where they lead their associations and members. Respondents were then asked how these unethical business practices affect consumer trust. Some examples of such unethical business practices are: foreclosure rescue and firm-specific illegal actions, crimes, and unethical business practices in the airline, automotive, banking and finance, electronic entertainment software, healthcare, petroleum, pharmaceutical, retail, and telecommunications industries. These industries have committed to engage in this variety of foreclosure rescue and firm-specific illegalities, are responsible for them, and are profiting from them. The information gathered in this round of in-depth interviews also indicates and assists with drawing an association between these unethical business practices and trust-determining and consumer trust constructs.

## 5. Findings and Discussion

In the information era, corporate reputation and trust are two of the most important assets a company has. Unethical business practices, in some cases, are hidden from day-to-day management and have been shown to badly affect consumer trust. This study investigates the impact of different types of unethical behavior, listed or not as criminal, and whether the profitability of the activities matters on consumer trust. A total of 390 consumers were surveyed. The results reveal a three-dimensional structure of the concept: behavior, profitability, and legality. Moreover, the results point out that the type of behavior has an impact on the importance given to consumer trust and the relevance of perception of the profitability of this behavior has on consumer trust. These results are of interest to both the private sector and its main stakeholders in predicting the consumer's response to bad news about a company. 1. Introduction Trust has, in recent years, made its way to the center of the corporate literature and now it is considered the foundation of organizations; a business mission, values, strategy, and organization are keys to a successful company. The complexity of the world in which organizations operate is leading to a series of new risk games: in particular, as customers are more educated and informed, individual scandals can cause a drop in a company's share price and even bankruptcy. Corporate scandals, in addition to generating losses in the company, may signify, in some cases, a loss of confidence on the part of society regarding the company's viability. The loss of confidence that may originate from these scandals is the argument for this line of research.

### 5.1. Impact of Unethical Practices on Consumer Trust

Consumer trust is a valuable possession that influences not only the immediate business relationship but also long-term business relations. For some companies, broad customer trust may bring significant competitive advantages. Many consumer studies are directed to how customer trust may impact customer retention, satisfaction, and loyalty, yet there is insubstantial research into possible factors that may influence customer trust. Trust in a firm is difficult to build and very easy to lose. Ways for a firm to violate customers' trustworthiness include unethical behavior. Studies have shown that consumers react negatively to a firm's unethical behavior. Ethical issues have always been a serious concern in business. Today, firms employ many different advertising methods to influence their competitiveness and attract consumers. Such advertising is not always fair to consumers. Nowadays more and more firms apply such methods when engaging in illegal or unsavory practices. What also requires attention is that, within some sectors, the overall picture of ethical problems is worsening. For these reasons, more and more attention is focused on consumers and the ethical standard in business. Consumers use previous information (trust) that they have to identify future risks and opportunities. For the commercial transaction to flourish, customers must be willing to believe that the firm will keep their commitments and try to carry out their promises. A trustful corporation may enjoy an implicit reduction in transaction costs (like search and cost of information) over the long-term relationship. In the e-commerce sector with a high level of information asymmetry, customer trust is indispensable.

### 5.2. Factors Influencing Consumer Trust

Consumer trust is considered a crucial factor for the long-lasting, profitable relationship between a company and its consumers. Trust represents friendly, transparent, caring, and good-faith interactions that all responsible companies should pursue. Trust is a feeling of confident reliance on another party; it reduces perceived risks. It is a belief based on the trustor's positive expectations of the trustee's integrity, ability, and benevolence. Trust based on these factors can build and deepen the trustor's confidence in a trustworthy party. 5.2.1 Controls Control monitors the level of the company's ethical practices and creates the necessary checks to establish a healthy corporate environment that sustains ethical decisions, activities, and outcomes. Blowing the whistle, or reporting internal events, is a means of monitoring organizational compliance, and it is an important detection tool for internal unethical behaviors. Higher levels of unethical behavior may decrease trust and overall performance because of lacking these important components controlling unethical behaviors. 5.2.2 Trust in the Board of Directors A company's governance system is a fundamental value creator; an efficient corporate governance system enhances the reputation of the business. The board of directors is accountable for the company, monitors operations, and controls management under the principle of separation of power. The board's oversight role increases consumer confidence in company operations because bad management practices, bad conduct to appropriate profitability, could cause significant negative consequences. A strong board of directors maintains these expectations and ensures management functions fit into the company's ethical principles.

## 6. Conclusion and Implications

While previous research has documented current business practices as being out of balance, it is unclear if consumers perceive the practices as crossing ethical lines or if seeing these practices as unethical affects behavior. Moreover, although a number of calls have been made in the extant business literature to investigate marketing practices, only a few studies have been conducted on specific marketing practices firms engage in and their relationship to specific consumer outcomes. Thus, our research contribution is in fostering additional and extended research on how unethical practices actually influence consumer trust. In a time of unfolding corporate scandals, the value of understanding the consumer effect of business practices is immeasurable. The impact of unethical business practices on customers and, in particular, on consumer trust in firms due to these unethical practices is increasingly significant as firms miss the gain from visibility, cool logo design, and corporate image. Our research is a step towards a better understanding of how businesses value the effects of a business practice on consumer behavior in order for these payments to be made more equal. As businesses continue to downsize and ethical behaviors continue to erode, maintaining this fairness could be instrumental in using public policy, employee activism or influence, and, most importantly, consumer influence.

### 6.1. Summary of Key Findings

This study has contributed to the ongoing debate around business ethics, consumer trust, and marketing practices by identifying the unauthorized repair of products after a recall as a particular unethical business practice that affects not only those consumers affected, but also future consumers of the brand, in a B2B context. The qualitative interview approach enabled the exploration of trust from two perspectives: the level of a single consumer with various brands (trust propositions) and brand loyalty and the attitude towards business practices of future customer industries of the business. The identification of unethical business practices when it comes to interacting with customers is not unprecedented, but our research fills an important void in the literature by highlighting the implications for future businesses, after a recall. Superior brand customer service and repair are commonly understood to contribute to high levels of customer satisfaction, with the desired outcomes being brand loyalty, recommendations to other businesses, and subsequent business from those organizations, as well as new business from other industries. After a recall, especially where the product comes with significant financial outlay, often power to make the decision to either repair or replace the product is taken from the customer and rest with decision makers in management. It may be designed to appear that the brand is choosing to look after the customer. However, the current process, as outlined by interview participants in this research, can leave a legacy of mistrust, affecting not only the interviewed company in terms of their future attitudes towards the associated brand names, but also affecting the long-term willingness of other businesses (including competitors) to engage with the brand and provide future purchase meaning, loyalty, and high price baseline and premium prices will be difficult to either achieve or maintain.

Manipulative Persuasion Unethical marketing can employ manipulative persuasion techniques, like bait-and-switch tactics, which lure customers in with an appealing offer only to pressure them into purchasing a more expensive product. These tactics prioritize immediate profits over long-term customer relationships (Jones & Ryan, 2018). One common method of manipulative persuasion involves the application of false authority, where manipulators deploy scientific references to bolster their credibility (Johnson, 2018). By citing studies, experts, or institutions, even when irrelevant or taken out of context, manipulators create an aura of expertise that disarms their target’s critical thinking. Confirmation bias, a well-documented cognitive bias, is also exploited. Manipulators selectively present references that align with their agenda while dismissing contrary evidence, further reinforcing their narrative (Kahneman, 2011). This strategic use of information preys on the human tendency to seek confirmation of pre-existing beliefs. Moreover, the misuse of scientific citations and references in manipulative persuasion undermines the integrity of knowledge dissemination and erodes public trust in science. It jeopardizes the very foundation of empirical inquiry, which relies on transparency, honesty, and a commitment to the pursuit of truth (Resnik, 2011). The Link Between Unethical Marketing and Customer Loyalty Genuine vs. Incentive-Based Loyalty Genuine loyalty is a true, emotional commitment to a brand or product, often driven by a sense of identification and affinity. Customers who exhibit genuine loyalty are more likely to make repeat purchases, provide positive word-of-mouth recommendations, and maintain a long-term relationship with the brand. In contrast, incentive-based loyalty relies on external rewards, such as discounts, promotions, or loyalty programs, to retain customers. While incentive-based loyalty can drive short-ISSN 2411-9571 (Print) ISSN 2411-4073 (online) European Journal of Economics and Business Studies July - December 2023 Volume 9, Issue 2 31 term profits and customer retention, it often lacks the depth and emotional connection found in genuine loyalty. Psychological research provides insights into the factors that differentiate these two forms of loyalty. Genuine loyalty is associated with emotional attachment, trust, and brand affinity, rooted in the principles of relationship marketing (Morgan & Hunt, 1994). In contrast, incentive-based loyalty is linked to behavioral economics and operant conditioning, where customers develop loyalty due to the immediate rewards and benefits (Fehr & Fischbacher, 2003). Customers are enticed to remain loyal through the promise of tangible rewards, which can include discounts, exclusive access, or loyalty points. While this approach can stimulate short-term purchases, it may not necessarily create deep emotional connections or long-term commitment to the brand. The best example would be the customer loyalty programs which are designed to reward and retain customers. However, when loyalty is driven solely by rewards and incentives, rather than a genuine connection to the brand, it can be viewed as manipulative and transactional. This undermines the very essence of loyalty programs (Kumar & Reinartz, 2018). Impact on Trust Trust is one of the most important elements of the customer loyalty and unethical marketing practices can have a detrimental impact on trust. A study of DelVecchio (2015) demonstrated that deceptive advertising significantly reduces consumer trust in a brand, leading to a decline in loyalty and repeat purchase intentions. When consumers perceive that a brand is not being honest or transparent, their trust is eroded. Furthermore, Du et al. (2018) found that unethical marketing can lead to consumer scepticism and reduced credibility perceptions of the marketing message. This scepticism extends beyond the specific campaign and can negatively affect the overall reputation of the company. In the age of social media and online reviews, negative experiences resulting from unethical marketing practices can be rapidly shared, magnifying their impact. A single instance of deception or manipulation can tarnish a brand's reputation for years to come. Repercussions for Customer Retention Unethical marketing practices can have hard consequences on customer retention. Companies must make ethical marketing a priority and consider it as a strong foundation for building and maintaining long-term customer relationships, guaranteeing constant success and a positive brand image. Unethical marketing tactics can have a significant negative impact on customer retention. Customers who have experienced deceptive practices are more likely to churn, diminishing the effectiveness of loyalty programs aimed at retaining them (Verhoef et al., 2007). ISSN 2411-9571 (Print) ISSN 2411-4073 (online) European Journal of Economics and Business Studies July - December 2023 Volume 9, Issue 2 32 Results and discussions Consequences on Customer Loyalty Erosion of Credibility When customers notice that their loyalty is not rewarded but becomes a part of an overall strategy of unethical marketing, the reliability on the brand and on customer loyalty programs is compromised. Customers may become sceptical of program benefits, doubting whether the rewards promised will be delivered as advertised (Dowling & Uncles, 1997). Several entities, including; NGO-s, educational institutions, nonprofits, and media, rely on conviction and trustworthiness to preserve their integrity. When they engage in or associate themselves with unethical marketing practices, their credibility is at risk. A study conducted by Cherniss (2016) highlights the negative consequences of nonprofits partnering with corporations engaged in unethical marketing, suggesting that such partnerships can erode public trust and damage the reputation of the nonprofit sector as a whole. Diminished Customer Engagement Customers who have been exposed to unethical marketing may feel mistreated and cut connections with the company. Unethical marketing can lead to a decrease in customer loyalty. When customers feel deceived or manipulated, they are more likely to seek products or services from companies they perceive as more ethical (Grewal et al., 2017). This shift in customer loyalty contributes to reduced engagement with the unethical brand. Legal and Regulatory Risks Unethical marketing practices, especially those involving deception, can expose companies to legal and regulatory risks. These may result in fines, legal battles, and damage to the brand's reputation, further complicating the task of building and maintaining customer loyalty (Kaplan et al., 2016). In addition to legal consequences, unethical marketing practices can expose companies to regulatory risks. Regulatory bodies are responsible for creating and enforcing guidelines that govern marketing practices, ensuring that they align with broader societal values and ethical standards. Companies that ignore these regulations may face sanctions or restrictions on their marketing activities. Scientific references reveal that regulatory bodies are increasingly focused on the ethical implications of marketing. A study by Smith and Brown (2020) highlighted that regulatory agencies worldwide are becoming more vigilant in monitoring and regulating marketing practices to protect consumers and maintain market fairness. Conclusion The Imperative of Ethical Marketing for Customer Loyalty ISSN 2411-9571 (Print) ISSN 2411-4073 (online) European Journal of Economics and Business Studies July - December 2023 Volume 9, Issue 2 33 In today’s market game where information travels quickly and consumers are much better informed, the application of ethical marketing is not just a legal and moral responsibility but also cornerstone of business strategy. Ethical marketing practices are not only morally right but also strategically sound. On the contrary, unethical marketing methods can erode trust and loyalty and disconnect the company from the customers. When consumers perceive that a brand is not being honest or transparent, their trust is eroded and the reliability on the brand and on customer loyalty programs is compromised. Unethical marketing practices, can also expose companies to legal and regulatory risks. These may result in fines, legal battles, and damage to the brand's reputation. Companies that consider as a priority the ethical marketing tools will not only build long-term customer loyalty but also establish a more responsible business atmosphere. Recommendations Future researches should be conducted to analyse the perceptions of the customers related to the usage of unethical marketing strategies by the companies, and also to quantitatively study the impact of these strategies on the customer retention and loyalty. #### The Impact of Ethical Marketing on Consumer Trust and Loyalty

![Marketing Director - Brand Strategy, Market Growth, and Campaign Management | EOXS](https://eoxs.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/Marketing-Director-1024x579.webp)

Building trust in a brand is more than just offering quality products; it’s about ethical practices that resonate with the values of consumers.

In today’s competitive marketplace, where consumers have an abundance of choices, ethical marketing has emerged as a critical factor in establishing and maintaining consumer trust and loyalty. Ethical marketing refers to the practice of promoting products and services in ways that not only adhere to legal standards but also reflect a commitment to fairness, transparency, and social responsibility. This approach goes beyond the immediate goal of driving sales—it cultivates long-term relationships with consumers by aligning business practices with their values.

## Defining Ethical Marketing

Ethical marketing is the practice of using honesty, fairness, and responsibility in all advertising efforts. It’s about ensuring that marketing messages are truthful and not misleading, that products are promoted in a way that respects consumer rights, and that companies are mindful of the societal impact of their marketing strategies. This approach also involves transparency in communication, ensuring that consumers are fully informed about the products and services they are purchasing.

## The Role of Ethical Marketing in Building Trust

Trust is a foundational element in the relationship between a brand and its consumers. When a company engages in ethical marketing, it signals to consumers that the brand is reliable and values integrity. This builds a sense of security among consumers, as they believe they are making informed decisions and are not being misled. Companies that are consistent in their ethical practices can build a strong reputation, which in turn enhances consumer trust. Over time, this trust leads to greater customer loyalty, as consumers are more likely to stick with a brand that they perceive as trustworthy and ethical.

## Ethical Marketing and Consumer Loyalty

Loyalty is cultivated when consumers repeatedly choose a brand over its competitors, often because they feel a strong connection to the brand’s values. Ethical marketing plays a pivotal role in fostering this connection. When consumers believe that a brand’s values align with their own, they are more likely to become loyal advocates for that brand. For instance, a company that promotes environmental sustainability through its marketing efforts not only attracts consumers who value eco-friendly practices but also retains these customers by consistently demonstrating a commitment to these principles.

## Case Studies Brands That Got It Right

Several companies have successfully leveraged ethical marketing to build trust and loyalty. For example, Patagonia, the outdoor clothing brand, has long been committed to environmental sustainability. Their marketing campaigns, which highlight their dedication to environmental causes, resonate with their target audience, creating a loyal customer base that shares these values. Similarly, The Body Shop has built a brand around ethical beauty, focusing on fair trade and cruelty-free products. This approach has garnered a loyal following of consumers who prioritize ethical considerations in their purchasing decisions.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Unethical business practices significantly diminish consumer trust, leading to negative perceptions and behaviors. This erosion of trust can result in customer boycotts and brand reputation damage.
When consumers perceive unethical behavior, their emotional connection to the brand weakens. This disconnection can lead to decreased loyalty and increased competition from ethical alternatives.
Transparency in marketing fosters trust. When brands openly share information about their practices, consumers feel informed, leading to stronger relationships and enhanced brand loyalty.

Establishing ethical practices creates a foundation for sustainable relationships. Brands that prioritize ethics and transparency cultivate lasting connections, ensuring customer retention and advocacy.
